posts.name: Daniel Schoeff
posts.cooked:
How much did you spend on wine in 2021?

  • <$3000
  • $3001-5000
  • $5001-10000
  • $10001-20000
  • $20001-50000
  • $50001 is nothing for me
  • $$$$ (spouse left me due to wine spend)

0 voters

Also curious to hear the average bottle price and if your 2021 spending was higher or lower than the previous few years.

link_counts.title: Wine spendings compared to salary / financial assets

options.html: $$$$ (spouse left me due to wine spend)

posts.name: Michael Morrell
posts.cooked: ~11k 130ish bottles. Down from 28ish and ~380in 2020

posts.name: Daniel Schoeff
posts.cooked:
~11k 130ish bottles. Down from 3x that for both last year.

I need to head in that direction… spending was up there but #of bottles was way higher due to all my baseman’s bargain hunting. I think the basement is full now

posts.name: Tom R W  \/\/@rren
posts.cooked:
In no particular order:
-70% of my cellar is CA and ‘18 & 19 were highly acclaimed vintages.
-Lack of travel & eating out during the pandemic left extra $ to spend elsewhere.
-Joined WB in Feb 2021.
-2020 & 21 were big exploration years for me.

Yeah, I spent more than usual.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
-Joined WB in Feb 2021.

Yeah, I spent more than usual.

Those two statements may be connected

posts.name: Joseph Grassa
posts.cooked: My collection has been in it’s growth phase and the bottle count has tripled since April 2020. avg bottle price has gone up about 20%. But spending went down by 34% in 2021 and I aim for a drop by about 75% from 21-22, since I now have more than enough to drink, especially as I am to decrease consumption. Let’s see if I can pull it off.

posts.name: Phil T r o t t i e r
posts.cooked:
2017 < 2018 < 2019 < 2020 < 2021
I joined the Cellar Inventory Reduction Plan thread on July 22nd 2021.

2022 will stop the trend. For the first time ever, I actually wrote down targets for 2022 (number of bottles and dollar amount).
posts.display_username: Phil T r o t t i e r

posts.name: John Flanagan
posts.cooked: Why isn’t “I have no idea, and I prefer it that way” an option? It also give me plausible deniability should my wife ever ask this question, which, thank god, she hasn’t.

posts.cooked:
My collection has been in it’s growth phase and the bottle count has tripled since April 2020. avg bottle price has gone up about 20%. But spending went down by 34% in 2021 and I aim for a drop by about 75% from 21-22, since I now have more than enough to drink, especially as I am to decrease consumption. I’m in effectively the same spot as you in terms of percentages.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
With no set plan to reduce the cellar, it’s happened naturally. Less than 2 cases of wine bought in total in 2021, well short of even $1k. I always anticipated that I would start reducing the cellar ‘sometime’, but after a drop in buying in 2020, 2021 has seen an even bigger drop.

I still have plenty of good cellaring wines, plus an idea of the sort of earlier drinkers I’m happy to top up with.

posts.name: Paul Jaouen
posts.cooked: I bought very little and sold a bunch so on a net basis down about 325 bottles. I still have more wine than I can drink.

posts.name: Nick Rutk0wski
posts.cooked: Probably touched 6 figures. But boy will I be drinking some fire from here on out.

posts.name: Jim Stewart
posts.cooked:
With no set plan to reduce the cellar, it’s happened naturally. Less than 2 cases of wine bought in total in 2021, well short of even $1k. I always anticipated that I would start reducing the cellar ‘sometime’, but after a drop in buying in 2020, 2021 has seen an even bigger drop.

I still have plenty of good cellaring wines, plus an idea of the sort of earlier drinkers I’m happy to top up with.

This sounds like a good place to be.

posts.name: Dan Hammer
posts.cooked: I spent 25% more in 2021 than in 2020. Average bottle is $50.70.

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked: Where is the ‘basically stopped buying’ option?

posts.name: Brian Tuite  GOML!
posts.cooked: Far too much, my goal this year is 1/2 or less than I spent in ‘21.

user_status.description: Wine doesn’t have to be tertiary to be enjoyable

posts.name: Chris. Mc Cart
posts.cooked:
2020 & 2021 were 50% & 80% higher than the previous several years as I believe the red burgundy affordability/ attainability/ global warming trains are all coming to a point where I won’t compete or be interested in, so I re-filled what I could with '14, '15, '16 & 17 vintages. I’m confident this past decades wines will be, along with the previous decade - the meat of my cellar for my lifetime. I don’t see my trying to buy much at all from the CdN this year or in the future and perhaps only very small amounts from some preferred CdB producers.
I also don’t want a cellar where the highest cost wines will only start to become mature in 20+ years when I’ll be 70+. With new red burgundy purchases out, I suspect I’ll still buy a fair amount from elsewhere as I chase my preferred style of wines from a suitable climate but the costs should be considerably less. I should drop one level in the poll this year and for another few years, then another level after that when I should only need defenders.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: I spent more in 2021 than any prior year. Lots of reasons for that, but one of the drivers is setting up that “retirement cellar” that sets me up better 15 years out. Have also done a lot of backfilling and a little unicorn chasing. While pricing is up quite a bit, availability has appeared strong, I assume in light of so many strong vintages in so many regions that I buy. Gracefully, the economy was crazy strong last year.

posts.name: J0hn
posts.cooked:
I need to reduce the wine spending for both budget reasons (want to build a new house) and because I’m out of space but don’t want to add more until we get the new house. I’m trying to only purchase 2012 Dom (if I can get it at $175 or less), whatever Goodfellow releases on BD and in the spring and fall, and a couple of cases of Burgundy from Ansonia, mostly daily drinkers but a handful for the cellar.

Not sure if I can hold myself to this, but I wrote it down in my plans and goals for the year, which gives me at least a 10 percent shot at success.

posts.name: Keith Kladder
posts.cooked: Up significantly in 2021, primarily because of a spring trip to Napa, continued good De Negoce offers, and stocking of 2018 Napa vintage. Expecting a big cut in 2022
down significantly in 21 (especially on a net basis, did some cellar shifting) but price per bottle significantly higher

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked: Down from 2020, but up from all other years. The exploding interest in champagne, and drastically increased German purchases (increased over what was already a lot) are responsible.

posts.name: Todd French
posts.cooked: Interesting, my 2021 was down 50% from 2020, but slightly higher than 2019

posts.name: Todd French
posts.cooked:
Far too much, my goal this year is 1/2 or less than I spent in ‘21.

Your 2022 spend might be high…to fill that cellar!

posts.cooked:
66% lower in 2021 compared to 2020.
85% less than my all time high.

Most likely will be at this level or lower, as I’ve basically cut retail (save for the occasional lower-cost sparkler) and am sticking with two direct to consumer wineries.

posts.cooked: Interested to hear what our $50k+ club is spending per bottle on average… care to entertain it?

posts.name: Brian Pinci
posts.cooked: Whoa! I just looked and it was much much higher than I expected. It was a high year for me too since I’m about to retire and wanted to beef up the cellar.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
I need to start using the “my collection is in its growth phase” line. Somehow it makes anything seem acceptable.

I’ve been saying that for a few years now!

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
Why isn’t “I have no idea, and I prefer it that way” an option? It also give me plausible deniability should my wife ever ask this question, which, thank god, she hasn’t.

This is totally me.

I have plenty of respect for people who budget and track, and then on top of that each of us has different financial situations. But just speaking for myself and with no judgment of anyone else, the hobby is more enjoyable and less stressful when I don’t keep track of the dollars, the numbers of bottles, the rise and fall of my collection size, and all of that.

On a smaller point, I also agree that it’s nice not to be able to answer the probing and critical questions about it.

posts.name: Peter Hickner
posts.cooked:
For at least five years I’ve been intending to start shrinking my wine inventory. I have yet to make a dent.
Covid has not helped. About half the wine I open used to be consumed at group dinners with family or friends, or parties. Not in the last couple of years.
It also has not helped that other dining, entertainment, and travel expenses have been reduced, so it doesn’t seem like I’m spending much money.
This year, forces like smoke taint, prices that seem outrageous, and utter lack of space are going to make me reduce my stock to what I can fit in the wine cellar. I swear!

posts.name: Daniel Schoeff
posts.cooked:
Whoa! I just looked and it was much much higher than I expected. It was a high year for me too since I’m about to retire and wanted to beef up the cellar.

Yeah… I never looked until this year and got very red in the face (ashamed)!
